Alright, I want to welcome everybody. I think this should be a fun session today. We are continuing our conversation about partnering. And today, I'm trying to make sure that in each session, we're making sure that we have concepts for both sides of the partnering equation, both the company owner and the copywriter, that we're talking about more than just deal making, but also the actual work that needs to get done in order to make partnering work. And last time we talked about doing the deal and making, you know, getting the contracts done and it was helpful. And this time, we're going to kind of try to take the next step a little bit. Look at some of the things that I do when I come into a company and begin working with them for the first time. And the things that we're going to talk about today are especially important for business owners as well as copywriters because a lot of business owners will look at these kinds of recommendations and say, well, you know, I should be doing all of this for my company anyway. So I think there'll be value there. And we're going to be talking about essentially how to find the hidden value in a company. And that's how we're making a big splash as copywriters or rain makers as we go to work with a company. And it's important that the most critical days of that new relationship really are at the very beginning at the time when the copywriter and the partner are both, you know, the business owners thinking, wow, I'm giving this guy a chunk of my growth or a chunk of my profits in terms of royalties or commissions. I sure hope that was a good decision, you know, and that that is still to be seen, of course. And the copywriter or rain maker is thinking, wow, you know, I sure hope I can make this company grow and that the revenues I'll realize from this will make all of this worthwhile. There are other reasons why the first 60 days of a partnership are so important. First of all, the first things that you do with a new company, I'm going to speak primarily from copywriter standpoint today because most of us that are on the call are copywriters. But it's important, it's essential really for copywriters to make sure that that first 60 days on board really below the company owner away in terms of results for several reasons. The first is credibility. You know, you're creating a foundation here and first impressions are everything. And if you can get into the company owner's mind the picture of how much money is to be made together as partners in this deal, you'll have more credibility and the new ideas that you bring to him after that first 60 days will have more traction with them and they'll be more likely to be implemented. Another reason is it gives you power in the relationship. One of the problems that invariably arises between rain makers and their clients is the staff. You're the stranger from out of town. You're the outsider to this company and the people within the company have been busy building their own kingdoms, advancing their careers and all of a sudden here's a guy from outside who's coming in with ideas and copy and all of these other things. And so from time to time you'll encounter resistance from the staff and if you've established credibility with a company owner right off the bat the first 60 days by hitting some home runs the business owner is going to be more likely to be your advocate to his staff and to ensure that your ideas and your suggestions are seriously considered and implemented. And another reason is obvious, the money. You want to generate the greatest amount of money and the shortest amount of time for both of you. That's how we keep score. That's how we measure. So, you know, it's important for the company owner but for the copywriter or rain maker the marketing consultant or business builder that comes in to a company for the first time. You're dealing with a virgin list in many cases, a customer file that has not had optimally designed, conceived, designed and executed, promotion sent to them before. So, it's at that moment in time when that list is likely to be the most productive, likely to get the biggest amount of money per name on that file. And so, it's a key opportunity for the rain maker to go in there and make a lot of money really, really fast. So credibility, power and money for both of you so that first 60 days is really critical. So, when I go into a company and I look around, the first thing I recognize is that in every company I go to there are tremendous opportunities. Some of them are from things they should be doing that they don't. Some are from things that they're doing poorly or not at all. And just others are unrealized opportunities in the case of new products, stronger promotions, and so forth. And these, we've talked about this before, there's six primary growth engines in every company. You can go into a company and create new products or entire new product lines. That will add to the bottom line. You can go in there and you can bring in hundreds of thousands of new customers. Obviously, a big revenue increase. You can create campaigns and long-term strategies, annual strategies to cause existing customers to order more frequently. You can create strategies and promotions designed to help each customer make a larger purchase each time they order, thereby increasing the overall value of the file and, of course, the company itself. You can create promotions to keep customers with you longer and keep them active longer. You can improve the margins, the profit margin at the company by lowering the cost on some of the things they do, including marketing, fulfillment, premiums, and other things too. For me, customer lifetime extension is really important. A lot of guys in my industry, for example, will pay $169 to buy a new subscriber to a financial newsletter. They essentially go into the mail, they spend $169 for each new customer that they bring in. They're looking to break even on that for sale, but still that $169 is the risk. Of course, that's a rule of thumb. It varies some, but you get the general idea. Very few of them are willing to pay $169 to keep a customer. There are tremendous opportunities in customer retention. Let's see where we go next. Before I move on, let me just say that, and I've written about this, so I'm not going to spend a lot of time on it, but you can imagine, you don't have to be the greatest copywriter in the world, and you don't have to be the greatest marketing genius in the world to double a company very quickly, to double sales at a company very quickly. You can see how if you just did each one of these things 20% better, you brought in 20% new product ideas, or actually helped execute 20% more new products in the year ahead. Let's say the guy's got five newsletters now, you introduced a new idea for one new newsletter. You bring in 20% more customers, which could be easily done, even if the guy was just marketing through direct mail, by increasing the frequency of the mailing and the response rate on those mailings. You increase the number of times each customer orders per year by 20%. You increase the average order size by 20%. You see what's happening here? You keep each customer with you 20% longer, and at the same time lower the margin, lower the cost of fulfilling on each order by 20%. If you did all of that, you've got more than a double in a year. It takes a systematic approach to working through the company and consciously saying, okay, how can we expand the product line? How can we get 20% more customers and go through this list? If you're doing this and doing it every single month, you're going to get there and you're going to create miraculous results. Pretty soon you'll be writing easings about how you quadrupled a company in a year. The things that you're doing, the things that we just looked at, really fall into three categories. The first one is incremental improvement. This is kind of like, here I'm talking about testing. It's constant improvement. This process never stops. The thing about testing is, it's kind of like how elephants mate. There's a lot of thrashing around and loud noises, and 22 months later you get results. You get a baby elephant. That's what testing is like. A lot of the tests that you do are going to be failures. In other words, you're not testing things. You're not testing, there's no guarantee that any test is going to work. In fact, the better your original promotions were, or the better your initial strategy was, the fewer tests are going to actually work. Most tests cost money in direct mail or any place where you have to buy your media, for example. They definitely cost money, but even on the internet they cost money because there's lost opportunity costs and so forth. Enable improvement is absolutely necessary, but it's a long-term project and it tends to be a substantial amount of time before you begin seeing the really huge results or benefits from all of that testing. Now, this is in a company. A lot of guys that talk about testing and the Taguchi Method and all that stuff are talking about one-shot internet-based web-based promotions or testing a headline on a promotion piece, that kind of thing. Even in that case, you test the headline today, it's going to be three to four or five months before you're going to roll it out at most companies. There's a time factor there. Having a lot on testing in month one or month two with a new partner isn't going to blow him out of his socks in terms of dollars to the bottom line. The second kind of improvement is additive improvement. This is kind of a layering-on effect. This is where, as time goes on, these things begin to become a very significant improvement to the bottom line. I'm talking about, and we're going to get into this more deeply in a moment, but you go into a company and they've got a great direct mail business, but they don't have a web presence. You add another layer, you add the web presence, and then later on you add a new product or two. Then later on you add a television infomercial. It's additive and it tends to be sequential. It takes time. Again, it's not like incremental improvement in terms of testing where it's an ongoing process. Additive improvement, there tends to be a burst right at the beginning of the process. I'll give you an example. It probably means it'll have to jump over a slide later, but I feel like I'm being a little vague here. I went into a health company. They had no website, no e-zine, no catalog, no package stuffers to go out when they ship their supplements. We spent probably three months creating all of those things for the company. We gave them a catalog first. We used that copy to create a website. We created an e-zine and we brought in an editorial person to do the daily e-zines to their customers. The e-zines always led back to the website so that people could order. All of that was like two and a half years ago. This month I received a $50,000 royalty a little bit from the catalog, a little bit from the website. It just goes on like that every single month. I get a royalty from this work I did two or three years ago. This kind of stuff is cool because you do the work once and you'll have some updates from time to time. The work is substantially done. It's like a little money machine sitting there generating dollars for pretty much forever. It's almost like an annuity. Whereas if you do a direct mail package, if you're only relationship with a client, if you're not partnering, you're just doing that front end direct mail package, remember what's going to happen. You write a promotion. You have to be to strong control. That's a big challenge. He's going to throw a couple of other copywriters at that control at the same time. You also have to beat them. There's another challenge. As soon as you get to control, if you're fortunate enough or smart enough to, he now is going to hire other copywriters every month or every two months or every quarter to try to beat your control. Every single one of those things means that there's a good chance that your royalties from that control are going to stop like now. That doesn't happen with this additive improvement thing. Catalogs, websites, package stuffers, billing stuffers, thank you series, renewal series or customer retention series, those kinds of things stay in place for a long, long time. He's not throwing copywriters at them every month. They'll continue ginning out the royalties for a long time. The next one is explosive improvement. Those are the things where you can walk in the company and 60 days later, walk home with a whole lot of money. We're going to look at each of these now. Also, when we get to the end of the presentation, just know that this is foundational for next month's webinar, which is going to go into greater depth on the first 60 days on each of the, I think there's six things. What I would usually do going into the company, let me just say that from my standpoint, these kinds of webinars are a little difficult to do because there's some of you that are working, are trying to sell a new car that your partner's invented or other people are selling cosmetics, other people who are selling real estate programs. I'm keenly aware of the fact that not everyone on this call is working in the same industry as I am. Giving very specific examples is difficult. You're going to have to think your way through this process. Our goal today is just to give you some guidelines for doing that. In the first 60 days, our mission is to produce maximum money with minimum work in the shortest time. The one thing I want to point out here is item number two there, the with minimum work part. It doesn't matter to me if I have to work 18 hours a day for the first couple of months with a new company. What I want to do though is I don't want to have to rely on my new partner's staff to implement or execute the lion's share of the work that I do because if the ball gets dropped anywhere that's going to reflect poorly on me because ultimately I'm going to be judged on the bottom line at the end of this period of time. I want as much as I possibly can have under my own direct control. I think that's an important discussion to have with the company owner at the outset just so that everyone is clear on this. I have a client that I'm doing a product launch for right now. The first thing I told him was before I go ahead and do this launch for you I want to clear that it's not going to happen on your server, it's going to happen on mine. That way I don't have to wait for his staff. I know that my work will always be their lowest priority, the things that I want done because their boss is standing right there asking them to do other things. I want to have the maximum amount of control and of course in the shortest amount of time. Those of you who have done this kind of thing before or have been on a number of my webinars or even read a number of total package issues probably have a real good idea where we're going with this. I think it's a good idea to kind of work through the whole process. Let's start with the incremental improvement which is testing. As you're going through the company and taking a look at the opportunities it's really good to actually do this in a systematic way and create a checklist and record every thought, every idea, everything you should test, every new idea you have for him or whatever. You can keep coming back to that every month as you're looking for what's my next project, what's my next project. The first item here is test new customer acquisition strategies. You have to look at how your new partner is producing new customers now. There's really only a couple of ways that it's done. One way is a one-shot promo. That would be like he runs an infomercial. He has spent X number of dollars to put that on the air that infomercial then produces X number of new customers. Or he runs and adds a newspaper or he does a mailing or does a radio spot. These one-shot promotions basically will, because they have a cost associated with them, in most cases, they're going to be a pitch of some kind. It's going to be an offer for a product because you're hoping that you'll get enough money back from that sale to pay for the cost of running the promotion. Now, the things that you'll want to test there if he's doing one-shot promotions is you're going to want to look at media and we're going to look at media more in depth in a minute. You'll want to test, but you'll want to test other media. You're going to want to test the offer and we're going to look at a whole bunch of offer structures. You're going to want to test price because we've seen a lower price produce better results in customer acquisition and we've seen a higher price on some occasions produce better bottom line results, better return on investment on new customer promotions. You're going to want to look at testing those things. The other kind of model really is a process model. It's more like the Internet model where you have an e-zine, you offer free reports for people who sign up for the e-zine and then you drive people to the page offering those free reports with a pay-per-click campaign or banner ads or you run an email list and you send out an email to a whole bunch of people offering a free report. Well, there are costs associated with doing those things. So, pay-per-click, you've got to pay-per-click. And so, your hope there is that over time, not on the first transaction, which is generally an opt-in to a free e-zine, but over time you will receive a sale, a purchase from that person that you just paid PPC or SEO or banners or whatever to generate. So, it's multi-step. Instead of just one shot, do a direct mail and get a guy in, you're more patient, you're willing to wait and the numbers can be bigger. Now, obviously, if you're doing process promos now, they require a very low cost in order to work or a very high price, high margin product. So, if that's the way you're going, you'll want to test maybe other media that are equally as cheap and or you'll want to test products that are higher priced or have better margins. So, there'll be a lot to test there. Now, if the client is doing these, in addition, you may want to consider doing these as well. That's kind of what I was talking about earlier with that health company. Now, when in there and all they were doing is one shot promos to bring in new customers, right? All they did was direct mail. They would send out a promo by the supplement. People would buy the supplement, then that person would be on their file and every month they'd receive additional promotions. We came in and one of the first things that we did after we improved the response rate on their one shot promos was we created a web presence which was a process acquisition kind of campaign. So, now we, in fact, I just got an email in my box from Human Events and it's that client inviting me to get a special report on heart health. And so, we added this to it. So, if he's doing this, you might want to test this. If he's doing this exclusively, you'll definitely want to go back the other way. A lot of guys today are doing just the web, but I just had an experience where I took a bunch of my web promotions for a particular client, threw them over to my buddy John McCrae, who's a great copywriter, and he's on our staff. And I said, here John, turn this into a direct mail piece. And this is a copy that was working on the web quite well. And John did it and gave that client the very best ROI he's seen in the mail in, he said, five years by using that copy over again. So, food for thought. Okay, the next thing you're going to want to do is you're going to want to test your offers and your offer strategy. Now, there are a lot of different offer strategies, okay, and we're going to go through some of them here. And again, this is like a checklist, kind of an idea starter that you can use when you're looking at your client's products. See, this fun, instead of somebody saying to you, look, I want a new direct mail piece, I want you to write it about this and I want it done in 30 days, you're able to go in and you're able to say, hey, look, why don't we think outside of that box? Why don't we just get outside of any box and take a look at the entire process, the entire marketing model and see what we can do to hit home runs on five or six or seven levels instead of just, you know, okay, so I wrote a better headline than the other guy. So it gives you a lot more chess pieces to play with. All right, different types of offers you can test. A loss leader or continuity offer is like what you see the computer professor do online. That's where he offers you a free lesson on how to run a particular program. You have to pay shipping and handling and what he doesn't tell you on television is that you will then receive and be billed for an additional lesson every month or so until you tell him to stop. So he's losing money on that first disk. Actually, he's not because he's charging an outrageous shipping and handling fee. In fact, I was talking to a buddy the other day and he goes, that guy isn't his product, isn't computer lessons, his product is shipping and handling. I think he gives $10 for order shipping and handling. So anyway, so that's the loss leader continuity offer and it works particularly well on any kind of broad market product where you have a huge prospect universe where your product is very cheap to deliver relative to the price that you're charging. And when it's like a subscription type of product, something that you deliver every month or every week or over some given period of time. The free trial preview continuity idea, think about boardroom. They send you a promotion that says, hey look. It's a really great book on health or taxes or personal finance or whatever. We want you to preview it free for 60 days and it's a soft offer. Send no money now, just return this card and we'll send you the book. And then if you don't like it, return it and know nothing. Otherwise, we'll invoice you for $29 or whatever the price is. It can also be applied in a continuity way where they say, okay, well, you know, we want to send you this free book or video or whatever. To preview and if you like what you see, do nothing and you'll receive the next installment each month and every month we'll bang your card for the appropriate amount. So this is different in that there's no shipping and handling charges up front and it also requires well, and it can be applied very well to continuity programs as well as to one-shot books. Next one is a free product with negative option. Now this is like what the record companies and the book companies do. They come to you and they say, select 87 books on this list or in this package. They're at the little stamp or whatever, stick it on the reply card or magazine for that case, for that matter. And we'll give it to you free. And then the only string is that from now on we're going to send you a letter every month telling you what the selection of what our selection of the month is, you know, like the book club selection of the month, right? And if you want it, don't do anything. We'll send it and we'll bill you. Otherwise just tell us not to send it or select an alternative. So that's what a negative option is. They're going to get it unless they tell you no, but they have an opportunity to tell you no. Contests and sweepstakes. Now this of course is the publisher's clearinghouse model, but it also works really well for renewals. One of the most powerful renewal. I think it was the most powerful renewal step that Phillips had in any of their campaigns was a contest, a sweepstakes. And you could, for renewing your subscription, you were entered into a sweepstakes where you could win a trip to Hawaii or whatever. And for companies with multiple product lines, like Phillips for example, where they had like 12 or 13 newsletters, they would have one sweepstakes, but it would be run for all of their newsletters at once. So they're sitting there with a half a million subscribers or whatever the number was and one of them was going to Hawaii. So the cost of the company was very low relative to the effectiveness of the campaign. And these hardboiled investors, these old curmudgens, these angry old white men that subscribed to Phillips' investment newsletters ate these sweepstakes up. I guess they had a streak of larceny in them or something because they just love the idea of copying a free trip or a free computer or whatever. And so always consider those, especially when you're looking at customer retention programs. And then six is a hard offer. And that's where you just simply say, okay, here's the product, here's the price, buy it. And it can be applied to books, subscriptions, clubs. One of the things that I want to point out with a hard offer, and this is something we've just learned in the last year or so, there's one sentence that you can add to your promotions, this one simple little sentence that will just rocket your customer retention. And it'll cut the cost of renewal or customer retention programs by 60, 70, 80%, one little sentence. And the sentence goes something like, as a courtesy to you, after you've presented your price in the offer, you say plus as a courtesy to you, your subscription will be automatically renewed from now on until you tell us to stop. One little obscure mention. Okay, if you're selling health products, say supplements, you say plus, you know, you're buying a three month supply and just have one little line in there that says plus as a courtesy to you, we'll send you a fresh three month supply every 90 days until you tell us to stop. And all of a sudden you've got automatic reorders happening all the time. It was part of their original agreement with you when they placed the order and it's a done deal. So, cool little tip there. We're using that now on everything that we do in the financial field and it's just wonderful. Okay, and then I added seven because it's basically also for a hard offer but for a single product, it works for both. And then a hard offer for multiple products which would be catalogs or even multi product mailings. There's something I'm wanting to try right now. I have a client as many of you know in the financial field right now and he's growing like crazy. In fact, while I'm doing this webinar, he's doing one right now for 12,000 investors that we've been promoting for the last week. He's doing a webinar for 12,000 investors with a member of the Rockefeller family about trading the 4X. And I expect that we'll do probably two or three million dollars as a direct result of that call over the next week or two. And I lost my place. I was getting so excited about that money. Oh yeah. There's something I want to do with him that I haven't been able to sell him on yet because in direct mail where you're paying $600 to send out 1,000 pieces, you're spending $600,000 to mail a million pieces. When the stakes are that high, we've found that you need a very simple offer. But when you're on the web, we have three product levels. Let's say we have three products, all of them dealing with currencies, how to trade currencies. The first product is a monthly newsletter that sells for $99. The second one is a weekly trading program that trades ETFs, exchange traded funds, that sells for $700. And there's a high-priced product, a $2,500 product that helps you trade options. And so I'm thinking, why not give people an offer, especially on the web, at some point in their life cycle, where they can tell us which of those services they're most interested in because right now we're looking at their past buying history and we're saying, well, this guy has never bought anything from us, so we're only going to offer him our cheapest product. Well, maybe the reason he never bought anything from you is because you've only offered him your cheapest product and he's not interested in buying, you know, once a month advice on currency investing. Maybe he would jump at a $700 service like crazy that helps him trade currency ETFs. See, there's a false assumption there that just because he hasn't bought anything so far, he must be broken and he must be a deadbeat. But he might be a multi-billionaire and he's bored silly with ETFs, but he'd be really happy to spend $5,000 on a Spot Forex service or $2,500 on an option service. So, you know, you want to look at those assumptions. Anyway, I'm getting sidetracked, another good aside, though. You're going to want to test media. Now, when I say media, there are different ways to go with this. First of all, obviously, you're going to want to test new selections in the current media that he's using. So, if he's primarily using direct mail, you're going to want to take a look at what list he's mailing and you're going to want to do a couple of things. You're going to first want to ask, are we testing or have we tested all of the most promising lists in that particular universe? And again, I strongly recommend that just for your own edification, if you haven't already done this, you go to a library, get a copy of the standard rate and data service for mailing lists and just sit there and study it. I don't know, I think it's fascinating. You look at that and you're looking at people making money. Handover fist. It tells you what they're selling, how much they're charging, how many new names they're getting every month. There's a lot of good information on there. And you can go to whatever category your partner is or your company is in and you can look at all of the other companies that are selling in that same market niche and you can then ask for a list of the mailing lists that that client has already tested along with the promotions that were sent to those files and analyze which of these lists might really work well so we can get his mailing quantity up. And so new selections in the current media that he's using. There's another way to go about this too and that is to look at the niche that he's mailing into now and ask, well, what other niches are so close to this one that we could probably sell well into them as well? If you had a, let's see, what would be a good example? If you had a newsletter on investment, well, actually we did this. Let's say you have an investment, a newsletter on health. Well, we looked at the people who were the very first subscribers to those health newsletters and we looked at the fact that there's a psychographic there. These are people who don't trust the establishment. They want to take control of their own life. They want to take control of their own future. They don't trust doctors. Well, gee, what other niche out there is kind of like that? Well, the answer was investors, people who subscribe to investment newsletters. They don't trust Washington to handle their money for them. They don't trust the pundits on CNBC, most of them who own their own hedge funds or mutual funds and have their own axe to grind. They want to take control of their own investments. Gee, I wonder if we tested this health package to people who feel that way about money if we might get a good response. The answer is yes, which is why today I just mentioned a client. I just received an email from a client of mine who's selling a health product, but he's sending it to a political magazine list, human events. Be thinking about what other groups of prospects are like your current group, either psych... mostly psychographically. They've demonstrated the same mindset that your current customers have. So that's two ways to test media. In a medium you're already using, like for example, direct mail. You can see how that might work, for example, with television too. You're doing health promotions on do-yourself health. And you think, gee, I wonder if this would work on CNBC for the same reason and financial channel. So, next is to test new types of media. I've done a lot of this and it is a little bit dicey, but when it works it's just dynamite. If it's working well for you online, take it to direct mail. Same copy, same offer, same product. If it's working well for you in direct mail, consider taking it to television. Look at other media. I took a 16-page sales letter one time, cut it down into a two-full-page ad for USA Today. The ad cost, if I remember right, $30,000. I bought it through a remnant broker. So I got a good deal on it, but it was still fairly expensive. But it was like a center spread in USA Today. Most of my 16-page sales letter, or a good chunk of it anyway, was in there. Like gangbusters, we ended up buying every slot we could, not only in USA Today, but also other newspapers and tablets for that product. So look at other types of media. Another thing to think about there, video is all the rage right now on the internet. So we're all producing little videos for our websites. If I have a client in the health field, for example, and he's selling something that unstopps your arteries, I can do an animated flash that actually shows the product working in his arteries, right? And it can be a whole little eight or nine-minute presentation. Well, there are infomercials on TV that are working gangbusters that are nothing more than the same eight or nine-minute presentation strung together three times. So you have the makings. If it's working well online. You have the makings there of something that could work really well as an infomercial. So something to think about. All right. And then joint venture media. A lot of people forget to even think about this, but your client gets 600 advertising impressions a day. He's getting minimum. He's getting direct mail and email and even outbound telemarketing contacts from companies that he's done business with in the past and the recent past. So they're contacting him on a regular basis. They're also sending him bills, statements, reminders of one kind or another. And he's visiting their websites. Now they can be your direct competitors in some cases and others indirect competitors. In other cases, they just happen to have the same prospect universe that you do. And that gives you a couple of really great opportunities. And in the context of testing media, the obvious opportunity is to go to one of these companies and to do a deal to essentially wholesale your product to that company and allow them to sell your product to the people they're contacting anyway. Believe me, a guy who's doing billings or a guy does a billing to his file, that's a cost to him. He sees the cost of that bill being a negative. And if you can go in and basically say, you never have to pay for your billing again because I'll cut your billing cost in half if you let me stick an insert in there. You'll be surprised how many people will do that. And they'll also be happy if they have a catalog maybe to include your product. So look for companies that are talking to the same kinds of customers and structure some kind of a test to see if you can be effective selling to those customers, basically piggybacking on what they're already doing. They're also shipping products, by the way, to those customers. And getting your package stuffer in their product and allowing them to take either a percentage of the sale, which is beautiful for you because you have no capital risk, or paying them a fee upfront for the use of that space in their fulfillment package is a great way to go. Next you can test copy. And here are just some of the things that I test a lot. Cases are really big for me because I do write in the financial field. Right now, for example, we have a lot of things I could write about. If I'm doing a direct mail piece, I can write about the real estate crash. I could talk about the mortgage meltdown and all of the mortgage defaults and how that's going to affect the economy. I could talk about the crash of the credit markets, how loans just aren't being made today and how to make money on that. I could write about how the dollar is being gutted by the Fed and Congress to try to head off a recession. I could talk about and maybe sell something on currencies. Or I could talk about how commodities prices are soaring worldwide and how to make money on that. I can talk about the fact that China's economy is going to grow 10.6% this year, whereas ours is going to contract. So why don't you subscribe to our newsletter about investing in China? If I pick Theme A and another copywriter picks Theme B, and I write the best copy that could possibly be written on Theme A, but Theme B resonates with the market better at that given time, the other writer is going to win. If you're partnering with a client, it becomes incumbent on you to go into his customer acquisition promotions and to make sure you've got the hottest themes, hottest ways in possible in the mail all the time being tested. Formats, I just tested a 6x9 envelope package against a book of luck and the book of luck beat it by 30% worth of test. I also tested headlines at the same time and headline, let's see, headline A, which was my first choice, lost. Headline B beat it by about 35%. Opening copies should only be tested when you're testing a headline with it. You want to test things that matter. You don't want to test colors. You don't want to test tight faces. You don't want to test small differences. You ask yourself, will this change? Could this change in the promotion? Generate one additional new sale. Well, if you ask that question about a color, nobody is going to say, well, I'm going to buy this product because this headline was red, not green. So test things that really matter. Opening copy alone, no one's going to read it unless the headline grabs them. So opening copy, headline combinations are great tests. Premiums, pricing, payment options. Is it a bill me? Pay upfront? Installment? There's even a permutation that says, well, you can pay now, but we're not going to cash your check for 30 days. Look at the different payment options. There's been a great way to get cues for testing there is to look at who has used which payment options that you've already used on your file. Guarantee. We did a test not too long ago where we looked at our cancellations and refund requests. We saw that we had a 60-day guarantee, money-back guarantee. You get your money back 100% if you cancel within 60 days. But anytime thereafter, you get a refund on the remaining portion of your subscription. We looked at the data and of course we had this big huge spike in cancellations right around day 58-59. Those guys had made a mental note or maybe even stuck something on the refrigerators that canceled this product on such and such a date. After they got all of our free premiums, they got our two-months worth of service from us and then canceled and got a full refund. Our question was, okay, so that was pretty fresh in his memory if he just ordered 60 days ago. What if we tested a one-year guarantee against that 60 days and see what happens? Wouldn't people be less likely to remember that the deadline for getting their money back was coming up? The bottom line is net net, one-year one hands down. We got a huge drop in cancellations. They didn't come, not even a spike, not even a bump at 60 days. There was no increase over the regular monthly cancellation rate at the end of the year. That meant huge money for the client. So look at your guarantee, look at the terms and test them. Also look for urgency motivators. Deadlines are great, and testing them is important. Scarcity, a deadline is, if you buy before April 31st, you get a 50% discount, or you get a free gift, or something else. And then scarcity is another one. We're only selling 500 of these. That usually only works on very high dollar things. But there's a way to make a scarcity sell work on lower dollar things. We did it with Rodale, and it's called the Fast 50 Premium. Or basically, you say that the very first 50 orders we get will get a free blender. And it's just something ridiculous. I mean, this is on a $29 book. And so that costs a lot of people to want to be in that first 50. And I was always concerned that the ones that didn't get the blender would be ticked off and cancel. But that just didn't turn out to be a problem. Next is additive improvement. We talked about going online, adding an online component. Now, I just want to say this again. The cool thing about additive is, yeah, there's a burst of activity early on with a new partner. But once you get these things handled, they make you money forever. So here are some of the layers that you can add to your cake here. You can add an online component, website, easy, paper click, make sure everything is optimized for the search engines, for the spiders. You can add product stuffers. Remember, every single contact with a customer, I don't care what it is, it's an opportunity to sell. So when you ship a product, you should have product stuffers in there. One of the great things about product stuffers, one of the best product stuffers we've ever used, have been discount coupons. And for one client, we even did a calendar where every single, you gave them a calendar, beautiful calendar that you'd want to hang in your kitchen. And when you open it up to every single month, there'd be like five coupons in there for a discount on different products. And this was a health client. So month one was heart month, month two was joint health month, and month three was good digestion month or whatever. And then every month would have coupons for the products the client had that supported the theme for that month. And people were using those coupons, and it increased sales every single month when they flipped that calendar over. And that went out with every order. That calendar went out with our orders. So even just a simple letter with a coupon is a great way to get additive improvement here in terms of revenues. We talked about catalogs, excellent, excellent way to go. And customer service scripting, I mentioned this. I think it was two months ago on our webinar. But I love doing this. I just really enjoy it. I'll call up a client, a client's toll free number, and I will put that poor customer service person through their paces. If they only offer one and two year subscriptions, I'll tell them I want a four year subscription. If they say it takes two weeks to get my free gifts, I want them tomorrow. Can I get them FedEx? I'll just think of it. I'll be nice, but I'll just ask every possible question I can. And I've had customer service departments hang up on me. I've had them tell me that, no, we only sell one into your subscriptions. You can't buy a three year subscription at any price. And man, I'm typing like crazy. Well, I'm doing this, a report to my client, the company owner, giving him ideas to improve his customer service. And when I say scripting here, I don't mean scripting every kind of call they could ever get, but laying out broad parameters on how to increase sales and deal with the most common kinds of questions and problems they receive. If you can imagine, here I am, the copywriter who's making money by driving people to this 800 number and then having the people on the 800 line be totally incompetent. So I had a good excuse for jumping all over that one. The next item is customer retention programs. And on a customer retention program, it's amazing. You go into a company, for any of you who are following, we're on the slide. This is additive improvement and number five, customer retention programs. It's amazing how many really excellent direct mail and direct marketing companies I'll go into. And I'll ask them to show me their renewal program or their We Want You Back program or special incentive for customers to order just because at 11 months just before they become classified as inactive customers. And they show me stuff. And some of it's OK and some of it isn't. And then I ask them, well, when was this done? The answer is usually three, four, five years ago. Nobody's watching it. Nobody's testing it. Nobody's identifying which steps in these programs are working and which ones aren't. And yet that's the main profit center of many of these companies. You sell somebody a one-year subscription and you're doing it at break even. And then you get whatever you spend in the mail to get that new subscriber. You get that back, hopefully. Then you send them a $15 welcome kit and then you fulfill on their subscription for a year for a dollar a month or whatever that costs. And so you're at $27. You're in the whole $27. And the whole profit center of the company is getting that renewal. And yet renewals always take a back seat. And the reason is, even though that's where the money is, the risk is on the front end. And companies tend to be more afraid of risk than they are excited about profits, which mystifies me. But I can't think of any other explanation. Why would they spend millions of dollars, enormous amounts of staff time developing, new controls to bring in new customers? But they don't spend hardly any time or money on the back end making sure that every step of their retention program is as sharp as it possibly can be. So that's a big one. The next one is product ideas. And now, you come in with new products, new ways to add revenue for your client. Now with product ideas, there's a lot to think about. You're talking to a particular company, and you want to expand its product line in some way. You know that for every new product you add, that's a whole new income stream. It's a whole new profit center for the company. So it's a natural way to really grow the company's revenues and profits and to take a nice chunk for yourself. So there are several different ways here. So the first one is enhancement. That's taking a pre-existing product and adding some component to it that either makes it more valuable or makes it more desirable, more valuable so you can charge more, or makes it more desirable so you get a higher response rate. A good example of that is a financial newsletter that comes out once a month and it's a print newsletter and you get it in your mailbox, right? And it sells for $99 a year. But you want to enhance it. And so you add email trading recommendations on, say, currencies and commodities. And so in addition to this once a month thing that's like your grand strategy and it's your main portfolio and any monthly investment moves you need to make, you get these breaking news hot flash emails that say buy this ETF now. Gold is just falling. Great time to buy. Get in there. Or the euro is soaring against the dollar. Let's get into the euro by this ETF. So now by doing that we have two ways to go and we could test both of them. One is raise the price of the service. Instead of $99, now that's $199 service. So let's try that. The other way to do it is just to keep the price the same. And say in your promotion normally we charge $400 for this email recco service and you get it for free if you join now. So product enhancement is a way to go. Next is extension. That's where you take a current product line. And in my case it would be, let's say, supplements, right? And your client has a supplement that unclogs your arteries and it's going gangbusters. Well, the safest thing to do then would be to say, obviously we're building a large list of people who are concerned about their hearts. What are the other concerns about heart health other than arteries? Well, inflammation, which triggers heart attack and stroke, blood clots, let's create products that handle those other two problems for heart health. And then maybe just for good measure, throwing a co-enzyme Q10 product, which is for heart pumping action, the strength of your heartbeat. So that gives us three new products that are all very low risk because they're in exactly the same niche that's working so well for us right now. The same could be true in the investment area. The guy's got a newsletter about how to make money in tech stocks. And you say, well, why don't we add another newsletter then that's kind of like the next step. And it's on how to make money in options on tech stocks. So it's simply the extension. Again, it's a very low risk way to go because you already have something that's working in a particular niche. Next one is expansion. And that would be simply to take the product that you're already producing. This works especially well for, or maybe even exclusively for information products. But you're delivering a print newsletter. Well, let's reformat it and let people buy it as a podcast. Or let's produce it in video every month and allow them to subscribe to a video service. They can watch it online or they get a DVD. You're already producing the content. You've already proven the niche. You already know your marketing works. Let's look at other ways to package that content, to create other products that will be attractive to people in other groups. Now, one of the things to keep in mind there is that, depending on who you are, how old you are, and where you are, you prefer to get your information in different kinds of formats. So if you're a young woman, you're more likely to want to get your information in nice, glossy color magazines. If you're an old curmudgeon like me, a print newsletter is just fine. If you're a young techie guy, maybe you insist on getting your information on a blog or in some other online format. So the more ways that you can repackage that content, the more products you'll have, and the more market niches you'll be able to go to. So that's expansion. Then there's intensification. And this is what we do when we take a $99 service that gives you once a month stock records and then say, yeah, but if you want weekly recommendations on exchange traded funds that trade in this particular thing, currencies, commodities, whatever, that's $7.95. So you just bought the $99 thing. Congratulations. You're a genius. It's a great product. But do you know that if you're trading more frequently in these markets, you can make a lot more money? Well, OK, we've got this service that helps you trade ETFs. There's no leverage. The return is great if you do it right. And it's $7.95 a year. But look at how much money you could have made. And then at intensifying further by saying, well, since the dollar is crashing nonstop, the euro is going through the roof for seven years. It's kind of a no-brainer. Why don't we show you how to trade options on the euro or even trade the euro in the spot market? And we're going to charge you a fortune for that. But that's OK, because look how much money you could make. So you're intensifying the service. And each level, of course, you get fewer and fewer customers, but you're making a lot more money, I mean a lot more money because of the higher price. And it's justified because you're making more money. You're sending in more alerts. Sometimes daily, for example, in some kinds of services. So intensification, great way to go. Continuity and relationship, prestige, products, and clubs. I have a client where we took 10 of his services, bundled it into a club, a chairman's circle more or less, and sold it for $25,000. We made a lot of money. They also improved access to their guru. And Celebrity has an appeal that's worth money. It's worth paying for to people. So they get greater access to the guru they expect. And they get everything he publishes. And it's $25,000. And again, works great. And then finally, JV partner products. And this is kind of the reverse of what we were talking about earlier. Instead of just going to people who have prospects in your same niche and saying, hey, would you sell my product, you use that relationship that you established earlier on to go to them and say, hey, I don't have a product to promote this month, or I'd like to plump up my catalog or my website a little bit, why don't you also sell your product to me? I'll sell out of your inventory. I'll have no inventory costs, no product development costs. And I'll just take X% of the money. And I'll give you X% of the money. And we'll both be happy. So those are the things to look at there in the area of additive improvement. And then the final one is explosive improvement. And this is where I know that most of you are on to me. You know where I'm going with this. Because I wrote about it not too long ago. I told a story of Dan Rosenthal, who went into a client's company and said, well, what are you selling to your people on the back end? And he said, nothing. He said, renewals, that's it. And Dan said, well, you got 35,000 or 30,000 subscribers, customers right now. And you're not really promoting anything to them on the back end. And I think if we develop a product just for them, because they know you, because they love you, they trust you, it could be much higher priced. And let's offer that to these people. And so the guy says, OK. And Dan says, OK. But since it's my idea and I have to do all the work, I want 30% of the money we generate. You know, the net. And the client goes, oh, OK. And so long story short, $10 million in sales over a period of about, I think, a week to 10 days. Dan walks away with millions of dollars. The client walks away with millions of dollars. And we're great for everybody. Now, when you're going to, it's amazing how many opportunities like that you're going to find. I was talking to Bill Guthey of Guthey and Rinker. Several years ago. And their top product at the time was Vanna White's tooth whitening kit. And I said, hey, you run these great infomercials with Vanna White and you sell all these tooth whitening kits. What do you do to your customers next? And he said, well, what do you mean? They were making so much money on the front end that the front end was occupying 100% of their time. And they had never given a thought to what they were going to sell these people in month two or month three or month four. And you'd just be knocked out by how many companies are like that. And the cool thing is that it's the most successful companies that are like that. The ones that are struggling a little bit, they're already trying to eke out every penny of profit they can. But it's the ones that are going great guns and making money hand over fist. Those are the ones that tend to have the biggest opportunity on the file. Under the rubric of explosive improvement, we're talking about customer file promotions. Again, these are people who know you. They love you. Or your partner's company. They trust you. If you go to something in their field of interest, it brings value to their lives on the same level or greater level than they've already paid you for. You're going to get huge increases in sales in very short periods of time. Now again here, there are two ways to go about this. One-shot programs are campaigns. And basically, when a file is new and they haven't seen a lot of promotion, a one-shot campaign, anything that's out of the ordinary is going to produce tremendous results. But then that's going to turn into a business for you. And you're going to be doing it every single month. And the file will begin to become in newer to it. They'll get a little numb. They've seen it. They've heard it before. And so at that point, or even before, but with one client who was in that situation, I said, well, you know, to hell with this, send out one letter and hope we do as well as we did the very first time that happened, let's plan a 60-day campaign and just knock their socks off. And we're going to start with a letter and then we'll go to daily emails to them about that letter and to our very best customers, a FedEx package. And then daily emails about that package or about that letter to drive them to the product. And then we're going to reformat the letter as a mag-a-lock. And we're going to send that out at the beginning of month two. And we're going to make sure our newsletters and our easings and everything support this push. And we're going to focus intensely on this just for 60 days. Later, we came in and we added online events, webinars, video briefings, tele-seminars, and so forth. And I can tell you that today, something like seven years or eight years, nine years after that, after Rosenthal went in with that client and created $10 million on his first promotion, we're still fielding promotions. But they're campaigns, much more intense, that are bringing to end $10 million apiece. So it's doable. I prefer, because of the internet, because of the low cost of customer contact online, I prefer campaigns. And I know that if a tired file or a file that hasn't grown in a while is responding extremely well to campaigns, so much better than a one-shot, that I go with campaign strategy on everything. And so you're not thinking about just one promotion. You're thinking about an unfolding story. Now, we're now into the part of this where, obviously, these are the things, this explosive improvement. This is what you're going to want to do in the first 60 days. And any of you who have been with me for a while have heard me talk about horizontal and vertical marketing on a house file. Of course, vertical marketing is where you just hit the entire file. It doesn't matter how long they've been with you, how much they spend with you, with their recency or frequency or amount of average purchases. You bang everyone on the file with a promotion on a particular product. And usually, that's driven. The type of promotion you do is driven by some kind of shared experience or event or qualification. So on a vertical promotion, you're banging the entire file, everybody on the file gets the same letter, same offer, same price, same everything. Horizontal is when you stratify the file. And you say, well, send this only to people who spend $0 to $12 with me. And send this other letter to everyone that's spent $12 to $25 with me. And so forth until you've covered the entire file. So vertical promotions are driven maybe by product introduction or a holiday or a date. Horizontal promotions are driven by the customer, how he's qualified. This is only to people who have a birthday today or whose anniversary is today. And so if you weren't on the call when we went into great depth on customer file marketing and horizontal and vertical promotions, be sure to go and watch that video, because I think it'll help you a lot. And then there are a lot of other things that have proven explosive for me over the years. Number one is company positioning. The company positioning has created some of the biggest sales explosions that I've ever experienced in my career. Trying a new or different or celebrity spokesperson can create an explosive result. And then the rest of these items on this slide, media, offer, pricing, guarantee, delivery mode, order mode, fulfillment, customer service, joint ventures, are some of the things that I talked about earlier. Now these can be applied to your house file. In terms of media, let's get offline every once in a while. Let's go ahead and give them a phone call. Let's go ahead and send them a FedEx package or a letter, first class letter. Offer, the same thing. Let's try different offers with them or maybe stratify the file and offer everybody who in the past has preferred soft offers, a soft offer, and offer everyone who in the past has bought on a hard offer, hard offers. Pricing, same thing, stratify the file by the price, and so forth. And you'll see those items, letters A through K, in the PDF that you downloaded. So now we're down to the end here. So we have our first 60 days. What would I typically do with the companies that I work with? Item number one, which we're going to cover next, is going to be creating a powerful campaign for existing customers. And I'm going to take you through the product, the positioning, how to decide what kind of offer, how to set your pricing, all of those kinds of things. Number two, notice that number one is the only thing here that you need to actually execute in the first 60 days. And that's really good because doing it and doing it right is going to take you 60 days of your full time and attention or pretty much. But I think you can set aside enough time to get these other things done too. Now, creating a campaign that's going to blow the doors off anything that your client has ever seen for his house file is going to cement the relationship in wonderful ways. I've had clients give me cruises, gold coins, all kinds of wonderful things. And thanks for just doing that. But in addition, and that's going to give you the credibility and the power and establish the foundation for your relationship as we discussed, these next few things, though, are things that will then take it to the next level and set really the tone for the next year of you're working with that client. So the next thing is to create a comprehensive customer file marketing plan. As I said, I have a video on that. So if you didn't watch that webinar, I'd strongly suggest that you do. Number three, you want to create and improve customer retention materials. Now, on that, again, this is more a proposal or an idea document than it is actually doing the work in the first 60 days. But basically, to do the idea document, get it approved, get it in a pipeline. So that it's something you jump on as soon as you have this first promotion in the bag. Next, create customer service proposal. Ambush the customer service department, do what I suggested. Do everything you can to drive. You can think of to drive them nuts. Find holes. Maybe you won't find many. Maybe you'll find some you can drive a truck through. But anything you find in there, think about your client. He spends hundreds of thousands of dollars, in most cases, to acquire new customers or to drive people to that 800 number so they can order a product. And that's cash out of pocket. And if there's an employee sitting there who's too lazy or just not bright enough to turn that call into a sale, the client needs to know that. In most cases, it's not that they're not bright enough or not lazy enough. It's they just have them in given direction. And so ideas that you provide along those lines will be really valuable. Five, reduce delivery costs. Look at ways that you can piggyback deliveries in the physical world. If you're delivering a product, is there a way that you could also deliver that guy's newsletter that way in that month? Or is there a way to deliver his premiums online as PDF instead of printing them and mailing them and incurring that high cost? Think about all of the ways that you might be able to save him money. And there's a lot to be said there because some of this just has to do with spending smarter when he's doing his marketing. And some of it has to do with things that you can afford to do for free for this guy because you have a piece of the back end. So we'll be talking about that in our next call. And then finally, and this is what a lot of people start with, but it should be the last thing that you do in that first 60 days, is present the client with a new product development plan. The new product thing is pretty cool because once you've given him a whole bunch of ideas and hopefully prioritize them in terms of which ones you think would work best based on who his market is and what his marketing history is, then it's off your desk. Somebody else is going to create that product. You're the marketing guy. So somebody else is going to create that. So now think about the combined effect here on your client at the end of first 60 days. You came in, all guns blazing, created a promotion and put several million dollars into his pocket. Not only that, by doing that, he showed him a whole new business model in terms of marketing to his house file, where he can see that happening every month from now on forever. You think he minds the fact that you made a million from doing that? 10% of what you made him maybe. Secondly, you came in with great ideas for doing this every month and for keeping his customer longer and for making sure that every contact with customer service produces a sale or produces higher average sale. You saved him money in tangible ways that he can see. And you've given him a vision of his future with some red hot new products in the pipeline. So that's my first 60 days. All right, guys. We're an hour and 30 minutes into our one hour call. I hope this has been helpful for you. So thanks a lot. It was fun. I hope it was helpful. Take care. Bye-bye. Here. Bye-bye. Here. Bye-bye. Here. Bye-bye. Here. Bye-bye.
